channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: Climate Transition Risk Cuts Both Ways - Bank Policy Institute
item.description:
Since the adoption of the 2015 Paris Accord, global bank regulators and supervisors have focused on management of climate transition risk. Transition risk is the possibility that changes in government policies, legal decisions, technology or market demand may make fossil fuel or renewable energy technologies either more or less valuable. Bank regulators have almost exclusively presented transition risk as one-sided: green investments reduce transition risk. The emphasis has been on the risk that financing fossil fuel projects will lead to bank losses when government policies designed to reduce carbon emissions are implemented. For example, if a bank finances a fossil fuel project and the project loses value because the government establishes a carbon tax, the bank could face losses. Regimes targeting specified reductions in carbon emissions[1] such as those promoted by the Science Based Target Initiative[2] assume that transition risk only arises when banks are not moving their financing away from fossil fuels and towards green energy projects.

The risk that financing renewable energy projects could lead to losses has been almost ignored. But if a bank finances a green energy project and the project loses value because the government ended a critical subsidy, the bank could also face losses.[3],[4] Transition risk, therefore, is two-sided.

Bank Policy Institute research has consistently cautioned that transition risk applies to financing both brown and green projects. For example, a 2021 note pointed out that, “although never mentioned, there could also be systemic risk if green firms all did poorly at the same time as a result of …withdrawal of green energy subsidies by an Administration skeptical of climate change” and that “bank regulators should perhaps be hesitant to use the examination process to mandate shifts in asset mix for assumed but unproven safety and soundness or financial stability reasons.”[5] Similarly, a 2024 note concluded that “[r]egulators should not encourage or require banks to quantify transition risk by measuring the consistency of its credit portfolio with an emission reduction scenario.”[6] A 2025 note reiterated that “[e]mission targets used as transition risk management tools can increase risk since they assume that transition risk can go only one way—towards more decarbonization. However, transition risk can go in either direction—towards more or less decarbonization—at different times and in different countries. Making the wrong assumption can lead to losses.”[7]

When regulators’ scenarios assume that transition risk is one-directional, stress test results can become biased and discourage portfolio diversification. Whether this assumption is made as an effort to align supervisory practice with climate policy goals—or for other reasons—it risks underestimating exposures that could emerge if policy direction or market sentiments shift. A more accurate approach would incorporate transition scenarios with both upward and downward policy shocks, preserving the value of the analysis while also recognizing the inherent uncertainty of political and technological developments.

In this post, we review recent developments in transition risk, showing how two-sided transition risks have arisen in practice and led to bank losses on loans to green firms. Bank risk management departments must take a nuanced approach in managing transition risk, looking at the specific details of each transaction rather than assuming green investments always reduce transition risk. If regulatory authorities are to avoid increasing risks in the financial system, they should refrain from issuing guidance or expectations requiring banks to assume transition risk can go in only one direction.

Electric Vehicle Developments

As reported in table 1, after strong electric vehicle (EV) sales growth last year in most regions of the world, except for the large North American market in which sales declined 4 percent, every region is now expected to see slowing EV sales growth, including a sharp deceleration in Europe and a drop of 29 percent in North America.[8]

Table 1

Region Electric Vehicle Sales Growth 2025 2026 (Expected) Global 20% Slowing China 17% Slowing Europe 33% 14% North America -4% -29% Rest of World 48% Slowing

Source: Benchmark Mineral Intelligence

EV sales in China are expected to continue to grow, albeit at a slower pace, because of new sales tax incentive reductions and the reduction of subsidies. In the European Union, lobbying by automotive manufacturers produced a rollback of a requirement that tailpipe emissions be reduced by 90% by 2035, which would effectively ban internal combustion powered vehicles.[9] In North America, EV sales are expected to sharply decline, driven by the withdrawal of federal tax credits in the United States and waning government support for the EV sector.

Table 2 shows the average losses per EV for major auto manufacturers. From 2022 to 2025:Q3, analysis of data from auto manufacturers that report both EV sales and net revenue show that the cumulative losses for these EVs were $140 billion.[10] The average loss per EV over that period was about $25K. The Big 3 U.S. manufacturers—Ford, GM and Stellantis—took a cumulative charge of $52.1 billion from their pull back from EV production, which exceeded their annual profits.[11] For example, Ford took a $19.5 billion write-down, with $8.5 billion allocated to the cancellation of EVs, most notably the fully electric F-150 Lightning truck, which will be replaced by a hybrid model. Losses on EVs contributed to Ford experiencing its largest quarterly loss since 2008 in 2025Q4.

Table 2

AutomakerEVs Produced,

2022–3Q25

 Avg Loss/EV Ford 301,948 ($116,245) GM 374,963 ($38,670) Stellantis 1,313,000 ($27,418) Mercedes Benz 643,900 ($13,201) Volkswagen 2,610,500 ($5,938) Rivian 172,355 ($113,719) Lucid 31,107 ($340,438) Total 5,447,773 ($25,660)

Source: Bryce (2026)

Solar energy has continued to grow, with 2.2 TW installed globally by 2024. China installed about 300 GW in 2024 and is expected to remain about half of the global solar energy market until at least 2030.[12] Nonetheless, profitability in the industry has not been uniform, with notable bankruptcies in the U.S. markets and financial stress on firms in specific markets, such as Spain.

The United States Solar Market

Figure 1 depicts cumulative solar bankruptcies in the U.S. market between December 2021 and December 2024. During this time, there were almost 125 solar company bankruptcies.[13]

Figure 1

Source: SolarInsure

These bankruptcies have been caused by high interest rates, which increase the cost of financing solar panel installations; labor shortages of certified solar installers; intense competition; and policy changes. In terms of the final item, reductions in solar installations in California resulted from the change from Net Energy Metering (NEM) 2.0 to NEM 3.0. Under NEM, utility customers with solar installations can get credits for excess energy generated by their solar panels. NEM 3.0, put into effect in 2023, cut the value of the credits by as much as 75% relative to NEM 2.0. The reduction in the subsidy reduced the perceived value of new solar installations.

Community banks, mid-sized banks and large banks have all been listed as creditors to California solar companies that have gone bankrupt in recent years.

The Spanish Solar Market

Solar power now represents about a quarter of total power production in Spain.[14] The rapid expansion of solar power has led to frequent periods of zero or negative prices.[15] As a result, the value of operational solar plants in Spain has fallen from €916,000 per megawatt to €648,000 per megawatt, putting pressure on profitability and credit ratings of solar companies.[16] In 2024, solar manufacturer Soltec entered pre-insolvency and Spanish regulatory authorities suspended trading of the stock.[17] Analysts have predicted a wave of bankruptcies in the sector.[18]

Implications and Conclusions

These case studies have illustrated how the risks of investment in the renewable energy sector can manifest while the renewable energy sector continues to grow. Simply put, transition risk can go either way. In the case of EVs, even though EV adoption continues to grow, intense competition, policy shifts, and weak consumer uptake have led to large losses for automakers, increasing their credit risk to the financial institutions that finance them. In the case of solar power, the market is growing and strong in general, but if supply grows too fast, as it did in Spain, that could lead to bankruptcies and losses for the financial institutions that finance them.

These case studies only scratch the surface of the complexity of transition risks that bank risk management departments must evaluate. Regulatory authorities and external organizations should not define transition risk to be one-way only, or encourage banks to adopt emission targeting systems that assume one-way transition risk. Regulatory guidance that defines transition risk as one-way may only increase bank risk and increase risks in the financial system. Instead, regulators should encourage a robust transition risk management process that examines the special circumstances of each new deal. At a minimum, recognizing current political climate, climate risk scenarios should include cases where public policy moves against green firms and toward brown firms for many years before a shift back toward green firms. Fortunately, the recent Bank of England update to its guidance on bank management of climate risk[19] has recognized the two-sided nature of transition risk, a positive regulatory development.

The typical horizon used for climate stress testing of banks is 30 years. Even if it were reasonable to assume that 30 years from now government policy will be firmly set to combat climate change, a bank will still suffer a loss if a green energy firm goes bankrupt in the medium term. Thus, the conclusion that only green firms are safe from transition risk is warranted only if transition risk moves uniformly in one direction. At least one of the banks listed as a creditor to a bankrupt solar firm may have been running the Network For the Greening of the Financial System’s (NGFS) Net Zero 2050 scenario, which assumed “an immediate and rapid decrease of fossil fuel consumption (oil, coal and gas), and subsequent increase in renewable energy use (mostly solar and wind resources) government policy would shift uniformly in support of zero carbon emissions,” when making its lending decision.[20] It should be noted that the Federal Reserve withdrew from the NGFS over a year ago, however.

[1] See https://sciencebasedtargets.org/

[2] European Central Bank Banking Supervision, “Risks from misalignment of banks’ financing with the EU climate objectives: assessment of the alignment of the European banking sector,” January 2024, available at https://www.bankingsupervision.europa.eu/ecb/pub/pdf/ssm.bankingsectoralignmentreport202401~49c6513e71.en.pdf

[3] See https://sciencebasedtargets.org/

[4] European Central Bank Banking Supervision, “Risks from misalignment of banks’ financing with the EU climate objectives: assessment of the alignment of the European banking sector,” January 2024, available at https://www.bankingsupervision.europa.eu/ecb/pub/pdf/ssm.bankingsectoralignmentreport202401~49c6513e71.en.pdf

[5] Nelson, B., “Green Lending: Is Regulatory Exuberance Irrational (And a Little Dangerous)?”, Bank Policy Institute, 2021, available at https://bpi.com/green-lending-is-regulatory-exuberance-irrational-and-a-little-dangerous/

[6] Hopper, G., “How Should Banks Manage Climate Transition Risk?”, Bank Policy Institute, 2024, available at https://bpi.com/how-should-banks-manage-climate-transition-risk/

[7] Hopper, G., “The trouble with targets: Unintended consequences for the financial system and the economy. https://bpi.com/the-trouble-with-targets-unintended-consequences-for-the-financial-system-and-the-economy/

[8] See Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, “Global EV sales reach 20.7 million units in 2025, growing by 20%,” 2026, available at https://source.benchmarkminerals.com/article/global-ev-sales-reach-20-7-million-units-in-2025-growing-by-20

[9] Ainger, J, Krukowska, E, and Nardelli, A, “EU To Abandon Combustion Engine Ban in Win for Carmakers,” Bloomberg, 2025, available at https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-12-16/eu-to-abandon-combustion-engine-ban-in-win-for-carmakers

[10] Bryce, B, “Stellantis Writes Down $26B on EVs, Shifts to ‘Freedom of Choice,’ 2026, available at https://robertbryce.substack.com/p/stellantis-writes-down-26b-on-evs

[11] Subramanian, P. “Big 3 automakers took $52.1 billion hit from EV pivot,” Yahoo Finance, 2026, available at https://finance.yahoo.com/news/big-3-automakers-take-521-billion-hit-from-ev-pivot-142434779.html?guccounter=1

[12] International Energy Agency, “Snapshot of Global PV Markets 2025,” 2025, available at https://iea-pvps.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Snapshot-of-Global-PV-Markets_2025.pdf

[13] SolarInsure,”The Complete List of Solar Bankruptcies and Business Closures,” 2026, available at https://www.solarinsure.com/the-complete-list-of-solar-bankruptcies-and-business-closures

[14] Red Electrica, “Solar PV takes the lead in Spain’s installed power capacity,” 2025, available at https://www.ree.es/en/press-office/press-release/news/press-release/2025/02/solar-pv-takes-lead-spains-installed-power-capacity

[15] Ma, J, “Negative prices for electricity are getting more common in Europe and consumer costs have dipped—while Americans face rising energy bills,” Yahoo Finance, 2026, available at https://finance.yahoo.com/news/negative-prices-electricity-getting-more-095500403.html

[16] Jopson, B., “Solar power surplus in Spain triggers ‘discount season’ for plants,” Financial Times, 2025.

[17] Melina, P. “Soltec enters pre-insolvency as Spanish regulator suspends trading of shares,” PV magazine, 2024, available at https://www.pv-magazine.com/2024/09/27/soltec-enters-pre-insolvency-as-spanish-regulator-halts-trading-of-shares/

[18] Bloo Power, “Renewable energy projects in Spain are facing a wave of bankruptcies,” 2025, available at https://www.bloopower.com/news/renewable-energy-projects-in-spain-are-facing-85258963.html

[19] Bank of England, “Supervisory Statement 5/25-Enhancing Banks’s and Insurers’ Approaches to Managing Climate Related Risks” 2025, available at https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/-/media/boe/files/prudential-regulation/supervisory-statement/2025/ss425-december-2025.pdf

[20] Network for Greening the Financial System, “NGFS long-term scenarios, narratives and key findings: Net Zero 2050, Current Policies and Fragmented World,” p. 14
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Climate risks could hit sovereign debt and private markets - Pensions Age Magazine
item.description:
Pension funds must assess the growing impact of climate risk on both sovereign debt and private assets, Ortec Finance has warned, as its 2026 climate scenario update modelled the effect of physical climate risks on government bond holdings for the first time.

In higher warming scenarios, the firm noted, extreme weather events and climate tipping points would drive sharp and sustained declines in GDP, erode tax revenues and result in significant uninsured losses.

The latest figures showed that the costs of climate change alone could push the UK’s debt-to-GDP ratio to 114 per cent by 2050, up from 102 per cent in 2025.

Ortec Finance said this additional stress would increase sovereign debt risk premiums and put pressure on bond returns, as interest rates rise across the developed world and trigger downgrades to sovereign credit ratings in the short to medium term.

Ortec Finance managing director for climate scenarios and sustainability, Maurits van Joolingen, said: “In our view, the adverse impact of climate change on global GDP, levels of insurability and ultimately the ability of governments to plug the funding gap through sovereign debt markets has been an important missing link in the total portfolio assessment of climate risk.

“While climate risk is a systemic issue, it doesn’t affect all regions equally, necessitating a regional approach to investment strategies and sovereign debt portfolios, especially given their long maturities.

"It’s important for pension funds to evaluate how physical and transition climate risks influence national GDP, debt-to-GDP ratios, insurability and ultimately interest rates.”

Van Joolingen also warned that pension funds need to understand the physical climate-related risks associated with holding private assets - typically less liquid and held for much longer than equities or bonds.

“Investments made now into infrastructure or real estate typically have a 15-year-plus time horizon, so these are highly likely to be affected by rising physical climate risks,” he added.

These findings have implications for pension funds under pressure from the UK government to invest in growth assets as part of the Mansion House reforms.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Climate risk threatens REIT revenue, First Street report finds
item.description:
Physical climate risk is already cutting into real estate investment trust (REIT) revenues and could grow significantly worse without intervention, according to a report from First Street released Wednesday.

The analysis, which examined more than 45,000 properties held by 65 REITs across 61 countries, found that nearly all firms face some level of exposure to climate-driven losses tied to hazards such as flooding, wildfires and severe wind.

About 98% of REITs would experience revenue loss in a severe weather event occurring once every 100 years, the report said. More than half could see losses of at least 10%, while nearly one-third could lose more than 20% of annual revenue.

Jeremy Porter, chief economist at First Street, told HousingWire that the number will only increase — and the results will show up in company earnings reports and investor guidance. Climate-driven disruption costs the typical REIT approximately 1.1% of annual revenue, totaling an estimated $3.1 billion across the index.

That figure does not account for insurance, which is becoming more expensive and harder to obtain in higher-risk markets, leaving companies to change their approach instead of relying on traditional insurance policies.

Porter said that some REITs are “self-insuring,” meaning they set aside their reserves to cover potential losses rather than paying premiums to an insurer.

Broad but uneven exposure

The financial impact escalates sharply in extreme scenarios. A 1-in-100-year event would result in average losses of about 15% of annual revenue, while rarer 1-in-500-year events could push losses close to 30%, the report found. Across the index, a 1-in-100-year event would translate to about $43 billion in total losses.

“An investment in real estate is, at its core, a geographic commitment,” the report said, noting that unlike other asset classes, properties cannot be relocated to avoid risk.

Climate exposure is widespread but uneven, with a subset of REITs facing disproportionately high risk due to geographic concentration or asset mix. The report found that while most firms fall into a moderate risk category, a smaller group faces significantly higher potential losses — a disparity that broad market averages tend to obscure.

The U.S. dominates global REIT portfolios, accounting for more than 80% of asset locations and roughly two-thirds of rentable square footage. Many of the most heavily invested markets — including parts of California, Texas and Florida — also rank among the most exposed to climate hazards.

“We know that real estate is concentrated in your Miamis and your New Yorks and your Houstons — and, you know, some areas that we know have high levels of physical climate risk — but the markets that are still driving up prices and still producing returns that investors are looking for,” Porter said.

“I think as long as investors are watching places that are disproportionately at risk of physical climate risk, they’re there. They are likely to start to respond to risks more frequently in the future, but we’re already seeing them respond to actual exposure.”

Need for granular data

Wildfire currently represents the most severe financial risk due to its potential to destroy assets and disrupt operations for extended periods. Flooding contributes through frequency and geographic reach, while increasing wind intensity is expected to become the primary driver of future losses.

Looking ahead, the report projects that annual expected losses will rise more than 15% by 2056 under a midrange climate scenario if portfolios remain unchanged. In that case, average losses in a 1-in-100-year event would climb to about 18.5% of annual revenue.

“We know climate disasters are going to become more frequent. Even if we all stopped putting carbon in the air today, that wouldn’t change,” Porter said. “There’s just this momentum that’s going to take us out the next few decades so we know what’s coming.

“I think being aware, having the data, understanding that climate risk is material — but it’s not the end-all, be-all of your investment decision process — and just figuring out how to layer it into what you already know you are. You’re already looking at market investability, you’re already looking at demographics, you’re already looking at geopolitical risk. All these other indicators are really important. There is a piece that we’re missing in a lot of spaces, which is physical climate risk.”

The findings underscore a growing need for more granular risk analysis, particularly at the asset level, rather than relying on broad sector metrics or environmental, social and governance (ESG) scores, the report said.

First Street also found that REITs already incorporating forward-looking climate data into their investment decisions tend to have lower modeled exposure. These firms had nearly 7 percentage points less revenue at risk in severe weather scenarios compared to peers, although the report cautioned that the relationship is not necessarily causal.

“Over the last five, six or seven years or so, we’ve actually seen quite a bit of increasing sophistication and awareness around physical climate risk, but I still think it’s lagging,” Porter said. “I still think we’re at the very early stages of figuring out how to actually price it into equities and how investors may use that information in their decision-making processes.”
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Local banks face rising climate risks despite low exposure, BoT study says | The Citizen
item.description:
Dar es Salaam. Tanzania’s banking sector faces growing vulnerability to climate change, with risks concentrated in loan portfolios and collateral tied to climate-sensitive sectors, a new report by the Bank of Tanzania (BoT) shows.

The Climate Risk Analysis in the Banking Sector report, released in March 2026, warns that while current exposure to climate hazards remains relatively low, the financial system is increasingly susceptible to shocks from droughts, floods and other extreme weather events.

The findings indicate that banks’ exposure stems largely from the geographic concentration of loans and collateral in economically active areas, where climate disruptions could have outsized financial consequences.

According to the report, loans and collateral are predominantly located in districts with low to moderate climate risk. However, this concentration presents a structural vulnerability, as climate shocks affecting key economic zones could simultaneously weaken borrower repayment capacity and reduce the value of pledged assets.

“Climate-related financial risks arise from the interaction between hazard exposure and the geographic concentration of banking assets,” the report notes, highlighting the potential for systemic stress if such risks are not adequately managed.

The central bank’s analysis, which covered four major banks accounting for over half of the sector’s assets, shows that 59.6 percent of loans are exposed to low drought risk, while exposure to floods is even lower, with more than 94 percent of loans falling in areas classified as very low risk.

Despite this, the BoT cautions that climate change is expected to intensify over time, potentially transforming episodic weather shocks into persistent threats that could erode asset quality and increase non-performing loans. The report identifies agriculture, energy and infrastructure as among the most climate-sensitive sectors, noting that disruptions in these areas could directly affect borrowers’ ability to service loans.

It further warns that extreme weather events—such as prolonged droughts or severe flooding—can damage collateral, disrupt business operations and strain liquidity within the banking system.

“Floods and droughts can reduce borrower income, lower collateral values, and increase credit and liquidity risks, thus affecting the financial sector and the economy at large,” the report states.

Beyond physical risks, the BoT also flags emerging transition risks linked to the global shift towards a low-carbon economy, including tighter environmental regulations and changing market conditions, which could affect the profitability of certain borrowers and, by extension, banks’ balance sheets.

The central bank emphasises the need for stronger integration of climate considerations into financial supervision, warning that failure to do so could allow risks to accumulate unnoticed. Among its recommendations, the BoT calls for enhanced monitoring of climate-exposed assets, improved data collection on loan and collateral locations, and the incorporation of climate risk into stress testing and supervisory frameworks.

It also urges banks to adopt more resilient lending practices and align with emerging global standards on climate-related financial disclosures.

While the report reassures that the sector remains stable under current conditions, it underscores that continued vigilance will be critical as climate risks evolve.

“The banking sector has relatively limited exposure… however, monitoring is essential to cushion the sector against potential impacts of extreme climate events,” the report states.

Register to begin your journey to our premium contentSubscribe for full access to premium content
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: HSBC Faces Investor Pushback Over Climate Risk Accounting - Finimize
item.description: Large institutions urged the UK's accounting watchdog to scrutinize whether HSBC's disclosures underplay the financial impact of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Hay fever getting worse? Climate change could be the reason. - POLITICO
item.description:
Climate change is impacting Europe’s pollen season and aggravating people’s hay fever — one of the many health consequences of a heating planet detailed in a major study published Wednesday.

An earlier pollen season — which in the past decade has begun one-to-two weeks ahead of the 1991–2001 period — combined with higher pollen concentrations are exacerbating symptoms such as itchy eyes and throats, sinusitis and wheezy chests, increasing the risk of asthma attacks.

Worsening hay fever is just one in a series of escalating climate-related threats to human health identified by climate scientists in the annual Lancet Countdown report published Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: New FEMA flood maps could force Houston homeowners into 'survival mode'
item.description: JavaScript is disabled in your browser. Please enable JavaScript to proceed. A required part of this site couldn’t load. This may be due to a browser extension, network issues, or browser settings. Please check your connection, disable any ad blockers, or try using a different browser.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Six Takeaways from the IIF Global Outlook Forum and Spring Meetings
item.description:
From geopolitics and energy to AI, regulation, and market resilience, the week's clearest messages in six points.

Across the week, one message came through clearly: the global economy remains resilient, but that resilience is narrower, more concentrated, and more exposed than headline market performance may suggest. As the IIF’s Marcello Estevão noted in his Chief Economist Dispatch on Monday, the lesson was not collapse, but "constrained resilience" - growth that continues, but on a thinner margin for error and with fewer engines underneath it.

Taken together, the week’s discussions pointed to six broad conclusions that are likely to matter most in the weeks and months ahead.


1. Markets are holding up, but the disconnect with geopolitical reality is striking.

One of the week's defining tensions was the gap between market calm and policy anxiety. Adam Tooze captured it memorably in his live recording of Ones and Tooze last Wednesday: "Two worlds. For policy people, the world as we know it is really over. For folks in the markets, it's continuity - you keep on trading and you're looking for marginal advantages." That mismatch surfaced repeatedly across sessions on macro risk, sovereign debt, and geopolitics, with participants warning that markets may still be underpricing second-order spillovers from conflict, fragmentation, and persistent disorder.

2. AI is powering the boom, yet rattling the system.

AI was easily one of the most cross-cutting themes of the week. Kevin Hassett argued that "the AI revolution is bigger than the internet revolution of the '90s," and Morgan Stanley’s Ellen Zentner noted how quickly firms are already integrating it into day-to-day decision-making. Speakers and participants also focused on the uneven distribution of gains, the scale of infrastructure and energy demands, and the growing labor-market questions now coming into view. At the same time, the week also sharpened attention on the need for standard setters and supervisors to take stock of a new generation of AI risk, as the rapid emergence of more powerful frontier models brought cyber resilience, model governance, and critical-infrastructure vulnerability much more sharply into view.

3. Stabilization is not the same as normalization.

That distinction ran through nearly every discussion of the Middle East and energy markets. While the mood in Washington suggested some hope that the worst had passed, speakers repeatedly warned against mistaking calmer prices for a genuinely improved underlying situation. As RBC’s Helima Croft put it: "There is a limited window to try to avert a much more serious economic crisis." Paul Gruenwald of S&P made the same point succinctly saying, "There is no 'peace button.'" Energy prices may have retreated from panic highs, but shipping, insurance, and control over key routes remain under strain - a reminder that markets can price relief well before the real world returns to normal.

4. Resilience now matters more than efficiency.

A broader regime shift was evident throughout the week: firms and policymakers are no longer planning for a world defined mainly by efficiency, just-in-time systems, and benign global integration. They are planning for one shaped by resilience, redundancy, and strategic autonomy. LSEG CEO David Schwimmer warned that markets have been "trained to buy the dip" for a generation, but that this may look very different in a prolonged stagflationary or supply-shock environment. The point continued to come through across discussions on trade, energy, regulation, and debt. Resilience is replacing efficiency as the organizing principle of the global economy.

5. Digital assets are at an inflection point.

The week’s discussions made clear that stablecoins, tokenization, and digital financial infrastructure have moved from theoretical to operational. The core question is no longer whether this ecosystem matters, but how it scales and whether policy, market structure, and cross-border frameworks can keep up. Interoperability, legal finality, and regulatory clarity emerged as the defining near-term challenges. As Circle President Heath Tarbert put it, the GENIUS Act creates “a pathway to mutual recognition” of foreign payment stablecoin regimes — a notable step in a system that has “virtually never done that before.” Tim Adams captured just how quickly the conversation has evolved: “Two years ago at a member CEO meeting, half the room asked ‘what’s a stable coin?’ Fast-forward a year, and everyone had a product.”

6. The regulatory conversation has converged on modernization.

Another major takeaway from the week was the degree to which the debate has moved beyond the black and white conversation of more versus less regulation. The more relevant question now is whether frameworks are usable, coherent, proportionate, and internationally consistent. Fed Vice Chair Bowman was direct on what modernization is actually meant to deliver: "The most important thing we can achieve with this new approach to a framework is to make sure that we're allowing our banks to get back into that business of banking." From the European side, Commissioner Albuquerque offered perhaps the sharpest line of the week: "We agree on a common rule — and then want 27 exceptions — so it gets overly complicated and not necessarily as fit for purpose as it should be." The emerging consensus was that excessive complexity and cross-border divergence are not just operational burdens; they are risks in their own right.

Strategic competition, repeated supply shocks, fiscal strain, energy insecurity, digital transformation, and the risk of policy divergence are now defining conditions of the global economy. Taken together, the message from Washington was not one of alarm, but of realism: the world economy still has meaningful sources of strength, but they are narrower than before, and they sit alongside a geopolitical and policy backdrop that remains unsettled.

Members can view session videos of the Global Outlook Forum here.


 

In addition to the Global Outlook Forum, IIF Spring Meetings Week featured a full program of roundtables and expert sessions.

These sessions reflected the week's defining themes: the pressure to modernize regulation without sacrificing resilience, the accelerating buildout of digital asset infrastructure, the growing urgency of energy security and supply chain diversification, and the challenge of maintaining multilateral cooperation in an increasingly fragmented world.

In Conversation with U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent

  • Secretary Bessent returned for the second consecutive Spring Meetings, this year focused on development and the role of multilateral institutions. Core message: lasting development rests on growth, macroeconomic stability, and credible policy frameworks — the World Bank and IMF must return to their core mandates and resist mission creep toward climate and social goals at the expense of foundational development priorities.
  • Bessent praised the World Bank's pivot toward energy abundance and nuclear power, called on the IMF to focus on global imbalances and early intervention, and cited Argentina as a success story. His framing: "America First does not mean America alone." Notable quote: "The biggest risk to financial stability is a lack of growth."

Curtain Raiser Sessions — Neliupšienė · Smith · Greer · DeFilippo

  • Mandy DeFilippo (CEO, US, EA, MEA, Pakistan Standard Chartered): Characterized the global order as undergoing fundamental transformation — from a rules-based system to a transactional, power-centered one. A StanChart survey of ~400 clients found 63% identified geopolitical tensions and trade restrictions as the most significant driver of change in 2026; top anticipated consequence was a shift in trade routes. Trade flows rewiring permanently toward the Global South and local-for-local arrangements, as supply chain security replaces cost efficiency. On digital assets: StanChart projects stablecoins reaching $2 trillion market cap by 2028, generating ~$1 trillion in fresh T-bill demand — primary use case being cross-border payments in emerging markets. On regulatory modernization: strong support for the IIF's simplification agenda; inconsistent approaches across 100+ jurisdictions create systemic friction.
  • Jovita Neliupšienė (EU Ambassador to the U.S.): Welcomed Hungary's election results as a democratic signal and argued that moving more EU decisions from unanimity to qualified majority voting would accelerate decision-making on defense, energy, and competitiveness. On energy: Europe has cut Russian energy exposure from €140 billion (2022) to under €12 billion today, now sourcing ~75 BCMs of U.S. LNG annually — the green transition framed as an economic competitiveness imperative, not just climate policy. On defense: Europe is committing to a trillion-euro increase in spending over three years, but the market remains fragmented; the F-35's reliance on components from 11 EU member states illustrates mutual U.S.–EU defense interdependence.
  • Ambassador Julianne Smith (Former U.S. Ambassador to NATO): Explained European reluctance to join Hormuz operations — allies were not consulted upfront, unclear on strategic objectives, and operating under a trust deficit with the U.S. following tensions over Greenland, Ukraine aid suspension, and tariffs. However, European chiefs of defense have begun planning post-ceasefire escort and mine clearance operations. On NATO: allies signed up for 5% defense spending at last summer's summit — a North-South divide more than East-West, with Nordic, Baltic, and Polish nations leading. On Ukraine: Ukrainians have stabilized the front, gaining ground and deploying drone tactics reaching deeper into Russia; first robotic ground combat system deployed successfully. Key risk: 800 Patriot interceptors expended in the first three days of the Iran war — more than Ukraine received in four years — creating acute stockpile shortfalls. On China: urged closer alignment between European and Indo-Pacific allies to counter the China-Russia-North Korea-Iran axis.
  • Ambassador Jamieson Greer (U.S. Trade Representative): Outlined continuity in U.S. trade policy — highest tariffs on China, a global baseline tariff, and Section 301 investigations targeting structural surpluses. Trade policy reoriented from a consumer to a producer lens; success measured by rising real wages, a narrowing goods deficit (down ~17% since April 2025), and manufacturing job growth. Following the Supreme Court's IEEPA ruling, rebuilding authority through Section 232 national security tariffs (autos, steel, aluminum, copper). On China: talks "constructive and stable," focused on rare earths, agriculture, and aircraft. On USMCA: bilateral negotiations with Mexico more advanced than with Canada, targeting rules of origin reform. On WTO: "If the organization can't agree to do something that we've all been doing for 30 years, how could it agree to do something more challenging?"

IIF–FSB Summit on Fraud & Scams 

  • A whole-of-ecosystem approach is required — no single institution or jurisdiction can address fraud in isolation; public-private and cross-border collaboration is essential across prevention, detection, response, and redress.
  • AI and instant payments are fundamentally changing fraud heuristics — real-time data sharing is no longer optional; after-the-fact reporting is structurally insufficient in an era of instant payment rails.

Africa Roundtable · Sponsored by S&P Global 

  • The session assessed how geopolitical developments, energy shocks, and shifting global capital flows are reshaping Africa's macroeconomic outlook — idiosyncratic country factors (fiscal buffers, energy import dependence, institutional credibility) remain the key differentiators, not global aggregates.
  • Panel II examined debt sustainability, domestic capital market deepening, critical minerals, and digital infrastructure as the continent's defining investment themes heading into the latter half of the decade.

Cybersecurity Roundtable · Sponsored by Mastercard

  • Cyber risk has ranked #1 in the IIF/EY Chief Risk Officer Survey for 15 consecutive years — the Iran conflict reinforced this with a documented surge in state-sponsored attacks on critical financial infrastructure during the active conflict phase.
  • The central message: cybersecurity is not a technology cost — it is a systemic economic risk variable, and investment in resilience is a measurable enabler of growth and financial inclusion, particularly across emerging markets.

Physical Climate Risk & Insurance Roundtable · Sponsored by CFA Institute

  • Physical climate risk is increasingly influencing investment decisions, underwriting, and capital pricing across real estate, tourism, agriculture, and EMDEs — with diverging regulatory approaches across jurisdictions adding complexity.
  • Session 2 focused on mobilizing private capital through blended finance, MDB initiatives, and long-term institutional investors to address structural insurance protection gaps.

Regulatory Modernization Roundtable

  • Global regulators — BCBS, IOSCO, Fed, ECB, Bank of England, Canada — converged on the principle that modernization preserves resilience while reducing complexity; IIF called on the FSB to develop common Modernization Principles to prevent fragmentation across jurisdictions.
  • A key structural tension: asynchronous reform across jurisdictions creates its own systemic risk — increased compliance costs, regulatory arbitrage, and an uneven competitive playing field that can undermine the global leveling objectives of post-GFC reform.

G20 Cross-Border Payments Roundtable · Sponsored by Mastercard

  • Five years into the G20 Roadmap: progress acknowledged on interoperability, competition, and data standards — but persistent friction in last-mile delivery, compliance, sanctions, and fraud prevention remains the structural bottleneck.
  • Highlighted the importance of payments integrity and security being prioritized in any future evolution of the roadmap, in an effort to combat a significant rise in fraud and scams globally.
  • Digital assets are increasingly established in the cross-border payments landscape and may address longstanding pain points; IIF committed to publishing an updated G20 assessment by October 2026 (Annual Membership Meeting).

Market Monitoring Group (MMG) 

  • The session assessed current geopolitical and macroeconomic conditions against a backdrop of acute uncertainty — centering on the risk/uncertainty distinction: markets are pricing the most favorable scenario while policymakers are planning for a range.
  • Discussed near-term financial stability implications of the Iran conflict, oil price dynamics, and US fiscal trajectory — broad agreement that the divergence between financial market pricing and physical economic reality warrants sustained monitoring.

NBFI & Private Credit Roundtable 

  • Rapid NBFI expansion has made non-banks core financing providers across corporate lending, real estate, infrastructure, and EMDEs — raising structural questions about opacity, leverage, and systemic transmission, particularly through PE-owned life insurers.
  • A taxonomy problem persists: "private credit" conflates vastly different risk profiles — from investment-grade infrastructure finance to sub-investment-grade direct lending — complicating supervision and making systemic risk assessment structurally difficult.

Digital Assets Roundtable · Sponsored by Visa

  • Tokenization and stablecoins are moving beyond experimentation into early market structure formation — particularly in cross-border flows; BCBS risk-weight framework was criticized for perpetuating concentration risk.
  • Five open questions dominate the near-term agenda: programmable money unification, capital framework adaptation, clearinghouse/custodian roles, cross-border composability, and "Know Your Agent" (KYA) — a frontier challenge as AI-driven agentic commerce scales.

In Conversation: Claudia Buch, ECB Supervisory Board Chair

  • Chair Buch discussed current ECB supervisory priorities — EU bank resilience, geopolitical risk transmission, digital transformation, and the global debate on deregulation vs. simplification — affirming that Europe's banks have rebuilt resilience and the ECB will not allow that to erode.
  • Key message: simplification must not weaken resilience; the ECB's modernization program targets risk-based, outcome-focused supervision rather than across-the-board rule reduction.

Global Debt & Financial Stability Roundtable · Sponsored by Fitch Ratings

  • Global risk is at 2× COVID levels by IMF measures — markets remain bullish driven by AI and US financial exceptionalism, a disconnect that warrants caution and sustained monitoring.
  • Debt trajectory is unsustainable without policy change: Panelists agreed the current sovereign debt path is not a crisis today, but is on an unsustainable trajectory over a 15–20 year horizon absent meaningful policy action — with geopolitical spending pressures accelerating the timeline.
  • Architecture is sound; governance is not: Broad consensus that the international financial architecture has proven crisis-resilient, but requires faster decision-making, stronger political ownership, and expanded representation of emerging powers — IIF is actively advancing practical reform through the Global Sovereign Debt Roundtable's implementation guide on sovereign loan and ECA-covered debt restructuring.
  • Uncertainty — not just risk — is the defining challenge: Axel Weber framed the macro environment at '2× COVID' uncertainty levels, warning that while markets continue to price risk, policymakers must prepare for worst-case scenarios — particularly the risk that tariff-driven trade fracture spills into finance, the one truly global industry.
  • Governor Villeroy de Galhau received the IIF Distinguished Leadership & Service Award — his closing message: Europe's foundations are solid, but acceleration on the Draghi/Letta agenda is urgently needed to avoid falling further behind on productivity and capital formation.

Thank you again for your engagement with the IIF. We look forward to continuing the conversation in the weeks and months ahead.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Op-ed: The Mediterranean as Europe's climate laboratory - The Parliament Magazine
item.description:
The Mediterranean is one of the regions most exposed to climate change and one of the least politically prepared to address it. Warming faster than the global average and facing growing water scarcity and intensifying extreme weather, it concentrates many of the pressures that will define the coming decades.

For this reason, it should be seen not only as a region at risk but as a priority for climate action and a laboratory where the European Union can test more ambitious, coordinated policies.

The Mediterranean basin is home to more than 500 million people and connects Europe, Africa and Asia. It represents less than 1% of the world's ocean surface yet hosts a remarkable share of global biodiversity. Its coasts host intense economic activity, from maritime trade to tourism and fisheries.

Rising climate risks in southern Europe

The region has warmed by around 1.5 C over the past 40 years, accelerating in recent decades. Marine heat waves are more frequent, triggering mass mortality among marine species. Coastal areas, where a third of the population lives, are increasingly exposed to sea level rise and extreme weather.

Across southern Europe, severe droughts are affecting agriculture and water availability. The catastrophic floods in Libya in 2023 show the devastating consequences of severe events.

Heat waves are becoming longer and more intense, with direct impacts on productivity and public health, particularly among vulnerable population such as the elderly. More than 60,000 heat-related deaths were recorded in Europe in 2022, many in Mediterranean countries.

At the same time, biodiversity is under unprecedented pressure. A large share of fish stocks in the Mediterranean is already overexploited, while ocean acidification and rising temperatures are disrupting ecosystems that have taken centuries to form.

Time is running out. Applying a carbon budget approach to Mediterranean countries leaves little room for delay. A carbon budget defines the total amount of carbon dioxide that can still be emitted while keeping global warming within agreed limits, such as 1.5 C or 2 C.

The implications for the Mediterranean are stark. If emissions remain at current levels, the region's remaining carbon budget will be exhausted by 2035. Within little more than a decade, the Mediterranean will have used its entire share of emissions compatible with the Paris Agreement.

Climate commitments fall short

Avoiding this scenario requires transformation at an unprecedented scale. Emissions in the region must decrease by around 6% annually between 2030 and 2050.

This goes far beyond current trajectories and raises difficult political questions about how the effort should be shared. Depending on the criteria used, whether based on population, historical responsibility or economic capacity, some countries would need to decarbonize much faster than others.

Current governance structures are not equipped to address this reality.

Frameworks such as the Barcelona Convention, bringing together coastal countries and the EU, plans to tackle marine pollution, coordinate monitoring and establish protected marine areas across borders. But they remain fragmented, underresourced and insufficiently enforced.

Mediterranean countries face similar threats yet differ significantly in their capacity to respond.

While EU member states benefit from stronger institutional frameworks and financial resources, many countries in North Africa and the Middle East face structural constraints, economic dependence on fossil fuels or political instability.

Existing climate commitments also fall short. Taken together, the Nationally Determined Contributions of Mediterranean countries would keep emissions at roughly current levels by 2030, far from the reductions required to stay within the carbon budget.

Solutions for the Mediterranean crisis

If the Mediterranean concentrates the risks of climate change, it also offers the conditions to accelerate solutions.

Its natural endowment offers exceptional potential for renewable energy, particularly solar and wind. North Africa could become a major hub for clean energy production, while southern Europe could serve as a bridge for integration and distribution. Cross-border projects, interconnections and green hydrogen partnerships could position the region as a key pillar of Europe's energy transition.

Water management must become a central priority, supported by coordinated regional strategies, investment in water reuse, renewable-powered desalination and more efficient management of resources.

Protecting biodiversity is equally essential, through expanded marine protected areas, sustainable fisheries and reduced pollution.

Climate adaptation must also be strengthened. Investment in climate-resilient urban planning, nature-based solutions and social protection mechanisms will be critical to prevent these impacts from driving deeper economic and social instability. Countries such as Spain and Italy are already supporting similar measures, including wetland restoration and coastal protection, to reduce flood risk.

The Mediterranean brings together all the dimensions of the climate crisis: environmental vulnerability, economic dependence, geopolitical complexity and social inequality. That is why it offers a unique opportunity.

Cooperation across the region can protect its future and create models for others.

Turning the Mediterranean into a laboratory for climate solutions is a political necessity. If the EU and its partners act, the region can become a bridge toward the solutions the planet urgently needs.

Sign up to The Parliament's weekly newsletter

Every Friday our editorial team goes behind the headlines to offer insight and analysis on the key stories driving the EU agenda. Subscribe for free here.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: How Buro Happold is driving climate action across Boston
item.description:
How Buro Happold is driving climate action across Boston

From geothermal streets to net‑zero skylines, Buro Happold’s Boston team is reshaping the city’s buildings, infrastructure, and energy future – proving that urban growth and climate leadership can advance hand in hand.

Boston has always been a city that reinvents itself. A port turned innovation district. A research hub turned global biotech capital. A historic streetscape now racing to meet the realities of climate change. But beneath the cranes and laboratories and waterfront parks, another transformation has been unfolding, less visible, perhaps, but no less profound.

Over the past 15 years, Buro Happold’s Boston office has helped define what sustainable, climate‑ready urban development looks like, not as an abstract ambition, but as a commitment to improving the places where people live, work, and invest their futures. From the Seaport District’s net‑zero towers to statewide geothermal legislation, the firm’s engineers and advisors have consistently operated at the front edge of Boston’s energy and innovation ecosystem, translating global expertise into local impact.

What emerges from their body of work is not a collection of projects, but a blueprint: a model for cities navigating growth, climate risk, and the demands of the next energy economy, while delivering healthier, more resilient communities for the people who call them home.

A science‑driven city meets a systems‑driven practice

Boston’s rise as a world‑leading life sciences and tech hub has been told many times, but one of the quieter forces enabling that growth has been the rethinking of the physical systems that power it.

Buro Happold charts how the city’s innovation economy required more than lab space; it needed new transit, new energy systems, resilient waterfronts, and buildings designed for extreme performance. As tech and biotech expanded, so did the need for infrastructure that was as advanced as the discoveries happening inside. 

This is where Buro Happold’s approach, equal parts engineering rigor, regulatory fluency, and urban strategy, has shaped Boston’s recent trajectory. The firm has guided developers, institutions, and city agencies through the complex interplay of building performance, sustainability mandates, and economic growth, helping Boston grow without sacrificing its climate commitments.

Transforming the Seaport: from parking lots to one of the country’s leading net‑zero districts

No area embodies Boston’s transformation like the Seaport District. Two decades ago, it was asphalt and warehouses. Today, it is a national case study in district‑scale sustainability, resilience, and smart growth.

Buro Happold has been central to this turn‑around. The firm’s long-running partnership with WS Development has shaped planning, infrastructure, and sustainability strategies across what is now one of the most ambitious waterfront redevelopments in the United States.

Buro Happold’s work in the Seaport District helped create resilient streetscapes, energy‑efficient buildings, and cohesive public spaces that reflect the city’s climate adaptation goals.

But the jewel may be One Boston Wharf Road, Boston’s largest net-zero carbon office tower. The firm’s structural, MEP, and sustainability teams helped deliver a building that fuses high-performance envelopes with electrification, advanced heat recovery, and a resulting resilient system. This tower is more than a building; it is a statement of what the city expects from future development. 

The adjacent 111 Harbor Way and wider Seaport District planning further illustrate how Buro Happold operates not just project-by-project, but neighborhood-by-neighborhood, shaping energy systems, mobility patterns, and long-term sustainability trajectories.

Engineering the all‑electric city: Feasibility, demand management, and grid integration

As Boston accelerates toward a fully decarbonized future, one of the most urgent questions is how to electrify buildings without overloading the grid or placing cost burdens on owners. Buro Happold’s recent nationwide all-electric feasibility study addresses this challenge head-on, featuring climate-specific studies across multiple building types – from multifamily to office and labs.

Through detailed modeling across Boston, the firm evaluated:

  • five unique building typologies
  • electrification readiness
  • heat pump performance and sizing
  • peak demand profiles
  • capital and operational cost impacts

What emerged aligns with the firm’s published thought leadership: electrification is not merely a technology swap – it requires holistic design thinking across greenhouse gas emissions, grid resiliency, technology evolution, and life cycle cost. In defense urban areas, load flexibility becomes as important as efficiency.

Buro Happold’s Boston team has been especially pioneering in grid-responsive building design: shaping demand through thermal storage, smart controls, and load-shifting strategies that reduce peak electricity use. These approaches can lower operating costs, ease grid stress, and help ratepayers avoid future infrastructure upgrades. 

For Boston, where electrification is the cornerstone of Building Emissions Reduction and Disclosure Ordinance (BERDO) compliance and long-term net-zero goals, this work provides a technical roadmap for the next decade of transitions.

Boston as a testbed for energy transition: From HEET to GeoMicroDistricts to microgrids

Where Buro Happold’s Boston team stands apart is in its ongoing work at the infrastructure scale – the systems that connect buildings, neighborhoods, and utilities.

Massachusetts’ groundbreaking geothermal utility legislation, catalyzed by the HEET partnership and our GeoMicroDistric Feasibility Study, positioned Boston at the center of a national shift away from gas infrastructure. Buro Happold’s GeoMicroDistrict Feasibility Study was one of the first to rigorously model block‑scale geothermal networks across diverse urban conditions. 

Simultaneously, Buro Happold continues advancing microgrid and district energy planning across campuses, mixed‑use districts, and municipal facilities. The team’s analyses published in Retrofit Magazine, show how aggregated thermal and electrical systems can unlock lower emissions, improved resilience, and long-term cost stability. 

Collectively, this work is becoming a model for how U.S. cities can replace fossil fuel networks with equitable, shared, renewable systems.

A climate action plan built for delivery

The City of Boston is preparing to release one of the most ambitious climate action plans in the country – framed explicitly under a Green New Deal and a Climate Justice mandate. Buro Happold served as a key sustainability advisor in this multi‑year effort.

From the beginning, the city wasn’t looking for a report. They wanted a plan that could be delivered.

Buro Happold helped:

  • organize priorities across buildings, transportation, energy, and resilience
  • align strategies with BERDO, stretch codes, and net‑zero zoning
  • identify critical 2026–2030 actions where early intervention yields outsized impact
  • embed equity and community insight into decision‑making
  • design transparent data dashboards and tracking tools to support public accountability

One of the firm’s most influential contributions was helping the city unify policy signals so that building owners, developers, and institutions could see a single, coherent pathway across regulations. This integration is essential in a city where buildings represent the largest share of emissions.

By pairing deep analytics with community‑driven insights, Buro Happold helped shape a plan that is both technically credible and socially grounded – one that the city can actually implement.

A new architecture of compliance and opportunity

With Boston’s aggressive climate policies – BERDO, specialized stretch codes, electrification incentives – the real estate market is undergoing a rapid shift. Compliance is a now strategy. Resilience is now a valuation. Sustainability is now fundamental.

Buro Happold’s Boston office has emerged as a trusted partner for both policy-makers-and building owners navigating this transition. Whether advising envelope-first retrofits, electrification planning, or mapping out district-scale solutions, the firm is helping clients turn compliance into competitive advantage.

That same commitment to deep, place‑based engagement is reflected in the leadership of Buro Happold Partner Julie Janiski, whose work with the City of Boston and the Commonwealth of Massachusetts exemplifies the firm’s hands‑on role in shaping climate action on the ground. Julie has served as a consultant to the City of Boston supporting the implementation of the Building Emissions Reduction and Disclosure Ordinance (BERDO), as well as advancing decarbonization strategies within the City’s Smart Utilities Program. Her collaboration with Boston extends to multiple technical advisory groups, contributing expertise on initiatives ranging from BERDO compliance to Net Zero Carbon Zoning requirements for new construction and embodied carbon standards.

At the state level, Julie is an inaugural appointee to the Stretch Energy Code Technical Advisory Committee (SE-TAC), where she provides critical industry insight to the Massachusetts Department of Energy Resources (DOER) as it continues to evolve and refine the stretch and specialized opt‑in energy codes. Her broader civic engagement includes serving as a board member of Built Environment Plus and as an early co‑chair of the Boston/New England Carbon Leadership Forum, helping establish the region’s foundation for embodied carbon leadership.

Through thought leadership on high-performance design, embodied carbon, and thermal energy networks, the firm continues to push Boston toward a built environment that is equitable, efficient and future-ready.

Where Boston goes next

The story of Buro Happold’s Boston office is not just about sustainability or technology. It’s about shaping a city at a pivotal moment – balancing growth with climate responsibility, development with equity, innovation with resilience.

Across the Seaport’s skyline, the geothermal grids beneath city streets, and the labs and campuses driving Boston’s economy, there is a quiet consistency: systems that are designed for what the city will be, not just what it is.

Boston’s next chapter, one defined by net‑zero neighborhoods, thermal energy networks, resilient waterfronts, and a science-driven economy, will require exactly this kind of integrated, visionary thinking.

And Buro Happold’s Boston team is not just participating in that transformation. It is helping write the blueprint.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado
